title: Retentions, WIP, and the Cash Crunch That Hits Australian Builders Every July
excerpt: Construction and trades businesses often record their best June revenue figures and face their tightest July cash position. Retentions and WIP timing create the gap. Understanding the mechanism is the plan.
content:
The financial year closes on 30 June, and for most construction and trades businesses across Queensland, NSW, and Victoria, it ends with something to show. Progress claims cleared in the final weeks of June, a strong revenue figure in the preliminary accounts, and a project pipeline building into the new year. On paper, it reads as the year's high point.

What follows in July often does not feel that way.

This pattern, a strong June revenue figure giving way to a tight July cash position, is not a sign of a business in trouble. It is the natural consequence of how construction accounting works. The gap is structural, it repeats annually across building and trades businesses regardless of project size, and it responds well to planning that begins in June while there is still time to act on it.

Why Retentions Are Assets You Cannot Spend

In most commercial construction and subcontracting agreements, the principal or head contractor withholds a percentage of each progress claim, typically five percent, until practical completion of the project. That retention is held as security against defective work and released progressively as the project passes its completion milestones. On a $2 million contract, five percent represents $100,000 that the business has earned, the ATO has recognised as taxable revenue, and the bank account has never seen.

Across a business running several active projects simultaneously, the total retention balance can represent several months of operating costs sitting in the balance sheet under retentions receivable. It is an asset. But it is an illiquid one. It cannot fund a subcontractor invoice, cover a wage run, or settle the superannuation contribution due on 28 July for the April to June quarter. The EOFY accounts show the revenue. The cash flow statement tells a different story, and July is when the difference becomes impossible to ignore.

The WIP Timing Gap That Compounds the Problem

Work in progress creates a second layer of cash timing complexity. Construction work completed by 30 June is recognised as revenue in the current financial year based on the stage of completion, not on whether a progress claim has been submitted, approved, and paid. A trades business finishing a project on 29 June, submitting the final claim on 2 July, and receiving payment 30 days after that has earned the revenue in June and will collect the cash in August.

This gap is compounded by the timing of outflows. Subcontractors invoice on completion of their scope. Materials suppliers expect settlement within their trading terms regardless of when the head contractor collects from the client. A business with two or three projects completing in the final days of June may face subcontractor payables and materials invoices in the first week of July while the corresponding client receipts are still three to four weeks away. The business has funded its subcontractors and suppliers before its own cash has arrived. Most construction businesses running multiple active projects at EOFY carry this position as a matter of course. The question is whether the magnitude has been quantified before July arrives.

The July Obligations That Fall Regardless

July brings a concentration of financial obligations that applies across every industry but sits particularly heavily in a business already managing the retentions and WIP timing gaps above.

Superannuation contributions for the April to June quarter are due by 28 July. For a construction business with ten to twenty employees, this is a material payment that falls in the same month the new financial year's revenue pipeline is still building. If the business runs a mid-month payroll cycle, the July payroll and the superannuation obligation fall in the same two-week window while June progress claims are still outstanding. Monthly BAS lodgements for June also fall due in this period for businesses on that cycle.

A 13-week cash flow forecast built through June and projected into September makes this compression visible in advance. The retention balance, the WIP timing gap, the July superannuation obligation, and the estimated timing of progress claim receipts all appear in the same forward view. A June profit and loss that reads comfortably often shows a more nuanced picture when the cash is mapped week by week across the same period.

What Planning Before June 30 Changes

Construction and trades businesses that manage this cycle consistently share one habit: they quantify the July position in June, while options are still available.

Reviewing the retention balance on each active project and understanding which completion milestones fall in July or August gives a realistic picture of which cash is genuinely on its way and which is locked well into the new year. Examining work in progress against upcoming claim milestones identifies any projects where a claim can be submitted before June 30 to pull cash into the current year rather than the next. Where the timing gap is unavoidable and material, a conversation with the bank about a short-term working capital facility is more effective before the gap opens than after it has already affected the cash position.

A Working Capital Unlock maps where cash is committed across retentions, WIP, subcontractor terms, and supplier obligations, then produces a prioritised action list to improve the position before the pressure arrives. For most construction businesses, the exercise surfaces specific steps that reduce the July crunch in a meaningful way. ProfitPulse works with building and trades businesses across Queensland and NSW at exactly this point in the calendar. If your June revenue figures look solid and your July cash position has not yet been mapped, building that picture now is the most useful financial work of the next four weeks. faqs.q: Why does my construction business finish June with a tight cash position?
faqs.a: Because revenue is recognised when work is performed, but cash arrives when progress claims are submitted, approved, and paid. At EOFY, multiple projects often reach completion simultaneously while retentions remain uncollected and subcontractor invoices fall due. The result is a profitable June on the profit and loss statement and a pressured July in the bank account. The gap is structural and predictable, which means it responds well to planning done in June rather than July.

faqs.q: What are retentions in a building contract and how do they affect cash flow?
faqs.a: Retentions are a percentage of each progress claim, typically five percent, withheld by the principal or head contractor until practical completion. They protect against defective work but create a cash gap: the business has earned the revenue and the ATO has recognised it as taxable income, but the cash cannot be collected until the project reaches its completion milestones. Across multiple active projects, the aggregate retention balance can represent several months of operating costs tied up as an illiquid receivable on the balance sheet.

faqs.q: What is work in progress and how does it affect my builder's balance sheet at June 30?
faqs.a: Work in progress is construction activity completed but not yet formally claimed or invoiced. At June 30, it is recognised as revenue in the current financial year based on stage of completion, not on whether payment has been received. The cash converts only after a claim is submitted, assessed, and paid, which in most contracts takes several weeks to a month. A business with significant WIP at year end has earned the revenue in this financial year and will collect the cash in the next one.

faqs.q: How can I improve cash flow for my construction or trades business at EOFY?
faqs.a: Three actions make the most practical difference before June 30. First, review the retention release schedule on each active project and understand which completions fall in July or August. Second, advance progress claims on any project where work is substantially complete before month end. Third, where the timing gap is unavoidable, approach your bank before it opens rather than after. A Working Capital Unlock maps the full position across retentions, WIP, and supplier terms and produces a prioritised action plan.

faqs.q: When is the superannuation payment deadline for the April to June quarter in Australia?
faqs.a: Superannuation contributions for the April to June quarter must be received by the fund by 28 July. For construction businesses already managing tight cash from the retentions and WIP timing cycle, this obligation falls in the same window as the transition to new financial year billing. Missing the deadline means losing the tax deduction for that quarter and potentially facing the Superannuation Guarantee Charge. Setting aside the contribution amount as it accrues through the quarter avoids a concentrated cash pressure in the final week of July.

faqs.q: Does a 13-week cash flow forecast help a building or trades business manage EOFY?
faqs.a: It is particularly well-suited to construction because progress claims, retention releases, and subcontractor payments can all be mapped against the project schedule to produce a week-by-week cash view. The forecast makes the July compression visible in June while actions are still available. A 13-week cash flow build for a construction business incorporates project-specific timing rather than generic cash assumptions, giving the owner a practical forward view rather than a theoretical one.

faqs.q: When should a construction or trades business owner consider fractional CFO support?
faqs.a: When the business is managing three or more active projects simultaneously, the financial complexity of WIP tracking, retention management, subcontractor payables, and project-level margin analysis typically exceeds what the owner and bookkeeper can maintain without dedicated financial oversight. A fractional CFO arrangement for a construction business usually begins with cash flow visibility and working capital management, then extends to financial reporting that supports better project pricing decisions and forward planning.
